 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1z643","title":"Hardening Sweep: eliminate hard-coded constants, placeholder implementations, and frozen API versions","status":"closed","priority":1,"issue_type":"epic","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:04:45.686415Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-25T06:06:43.313858Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T05:50:03.501891Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["audit","hardening"],"comments":[{"id":1937,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-1z643","author":"jemanuel","text":"EPIC: Hardening Sweep (2026-03-25 audit by SunnyMoose)

A systematic code-level audit of the entire FCP workspace (28 core crates, 153 connectors, ~1.4M lines of Rust) uncovered several categories of issues that undermine production-readiness, correctness, and maintainability:

1. CRITICAL: Two connectors (DocuSign, Plaid) default to non-production environments (demo/sandbox URLs). Any deployment using defaults silently hits test infrastructure.

2. HIGH: Five connectors have placeholder implementations that look functional but are not: AWS SigV4 signing (affects AWS + S3), GCP JWT signing, DocuSign streaming, LLM-Router provider API calls. These pass tests but fail against real APIs.

3. HIGH: Four+ connectors freeze API version strings (Anthropic 2023-06-01, Notion 2022-06-28, Azure 2022-12-01, Salesforce v59.0) that are years out of date. Providers may deprecate or remove support for these versions.

4. MEDIUM: ~80+ connectors hard-code Duration::from_secs(30) for HTTP request timeouts with no environment-variable or manifest-based override. The infrastructure crates (fcp-streaming, fcp-webhook, fcp-sdk) also bake in timeout/buffer/retry constants that should be configurable per-connector or per-zone.

5. MEDIUM: Six connectors (firecrawl, huggingface, perplexity-search, tlon, zalo, zalouser) are scaffolded-only — they declare operations but return invoke_surface_unimplemented on all invocations. These need either implementation or honest demotion to 'stub' status.

6. LOW: Four TODO markers in fwc/src/bench_cmd.rs represent genuinely unfinished benchmark implementations (blocked on fcp-sdk, fcp-mesh, fcp-crypto Shamir). Template TODOs in fwc/src/new_cmd.rs are intentional and not actionable.

This epic organizes all findings into a dependency-aware task graph so they can be addressed systematically. The tree is structured so foundation work (configurable timeout infrastructure, API version management) unblocks the per-connector sweeps.","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:03Z"},{"id":1972,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-1z643","author":"BronzeStork","text":"Taking a narrow audit/fix slice under this hardening epic: the new fwc discovery/export validation path still panics on invalid operation/capability ids and silently drops invalid related capabilities even after the recent Result-based hardening.  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-1zw6","title":"[IDEA-23] Connector Lifecycle Manager + Canary Rollouts","description":"## Background
Connectors need safe lifecycle management: verified installs, health checks, and rollbacks. Current host supervision focuses on process control but not upgrade/rollback semantics.

## Goals
- Formalize connector lifecycle states (discovered/verified/installed/configured/active/failed).
- Add canary rollout + rollback based on health checks.
- Expose CLI/host APIs for promotion and rollback.

## Non-goals
- Full registry distribution or multi-node deployment orchestration.

## Approach
- Add lifecycle state machine in `fcp-host`.
- Track last-known-good version and restart history.
- Implement canary policy: promote after N healthy checks.

## Risks
- State transitions must be crash-safe; persist state in mesh objects.

## Success Criteria
- Host can roll back to last-known-good on health failures.
- Lifecycle state is observable via CLI/agent API.

## Acceptance Criteria (Refined)
- Canary promotion requires N consecutive healthy checks.
- Rollback reverts to last‑known‑good version automatically on failure.

-{"id":"flywheel_connectors-cchj5","title":"TEST-FUZZ-X: CanonicalSerializer schema-prefix binding + non-canonical-detection fuzz","description":"fcp_cbor::CanonicalSerializer::serialize/deserialize (lib.rs:265-376)
implements the schema-prefixed canonical envelope:
  out = schema.hash() (32 bytes) || canonical_cbor(value)

The deserialize path runs THREE distinct rejection gates:
  - MissingSchemaHashPrefix: data shorter than 32 bytes
  - SchemaMismatch:          got_hash ≠ expected_hash
  - NonCanonicalEncoding:    decoded value re-encodes to bytes ≠ input
  - TrailingBytes:           CBOR decoder consumed only a prefix
  - PayloadTooLarge:         data.len() > MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_BYTES

deserialize_unchecked skips the NonCanonicalEncoding gate. The
asymmetry between the two paths is the security boundary: untrusted
inputs MUST flow through deserialize.

Existing fuzz coverage (canonical_cbor) tests panic-freedom, JSON
round-trip via deserialize, and ciborium→canonical→deserialize
round-trip — all under matching schema. The metamorphic relations
that exercise the rejection gates are NOT covered:

  1. **Schema-prefix bit-flip rejection**: bit-flipping any byte of
     the 32-byte schema_hash prefix MUST yield SchemaMismatch.
  2. **Cross-schema rejection**: serialize under S1 MUST NOT
     deserialize under S2 (bound schemas distinguish content even
     when CBOR bytes happen to match) — load-bearing for the
     SchemaId::hash injectivity guarantee landed in mzi9x.
  3. **Truncated-prefix rejection**: input shorter than 32 bytes MUST
     yield MissingSchemaHashPrefix without panicking.
  4. **Trailing-bytes rejection**: schema_hash || canonical || extra
     MUST yield NonCanonicalEncoding (deserialize) or TrailingBytes
     (deserialize_unchecked).
  5. **deserialize ⇒ deserialize_unchecked agreement on accepts**:
     when deserialize succeeds, deserialize_unchecked MUST also
     succeed and decode to an equal value. The converse may fail
     (deserialize_unchecked is more permissive on non-canonical
     bytes) but the strictly-stronger gate must remain a refinement.

implements the schema-prefixed canonical envelope:
  out = schema.hash() (32 bytes) || canonical_cbor(value)

The deserialize path runs THREE distinct rejection gates:
  - MissingSchemaHashPrefix: data shorter than 32 bytes
  - SchemaMismatch:          got_hash ≠ expected_hash
  - NonCanonicalEncoding:    decoded value re-encodes to bytes ≠ input
  - TrailingBytes:           CBOR decoder consumed only a prefix
  - PayloadTooLarge:         data.len() > MAX_CANONICAL_OBJECT_BYTES

deserialize_unchecked skips the NonCanonicalEncoding gate. The
asymmetry between the two paths is the security boundary: untrusted
inputs MUST flow through deserialize.

Existing fuzz coverage (canonical_cbor) tests panic-freedom, JSON
round-trip via deserialize, and ciborium→canonical→deserialize
round-trip — all under matching schema. The metamorphic relations
that exercise the rejection gates are NOT covered:

  1. **Schema-prefix bit-flip rejection**: bit-flipping any byte of
     the 32-byte schema_hash prefix MUST yield SchemaMismatch.
  2. **Cross-schema rejection**: serialize under S1 MUST NOT
     deserialize under S2 (bound schemas distinguish content even
     when CBOR bytes happen to match) — load-bearing for the
     SchemaId::hash injectivity guarantee landed in mzi9x.
  3. **Truncated-prefix rejection**: input shorter than 32 bytes MUST
     yield MissingSchemaHashPrefix without panicking.
  4. **Trailing-bytes rejection**: schema_hash || canonical || extra
     MUST yield NonCanonicalEncoding (deserialize) or TrailingBytes
     (deserialize_unchecked).
  5. **deserialize ⇒ deserialize_unchecked agreement on accepts**:
     when deserialize succeeds, deserialize_unchecked MUST also
     succeed and decode to an equal value. The converse may fail
     (deserialize_unchecked is more permissive on non-canonical
     bytes) but the strictly-stronger gate must remain a refinement.
  6. **Round-trip preserves bytes**: serialize(v, s) → deserialize(_, s)
     → serialize(_, s) is byte-identical for accepted v.

Acceptance:
  - fuzz/fuzz_targets/canonical_serializer_schema_binding.rs
  - 6 properties + once-gated regression anchors for (a) bit-flip
    rejection on a known schema hash, (b) cross-schema rejection
    (SchemaIdA vs SchemaIdB on an identical CBOR payload).
  - Registered in fuzz/Cargo.toml.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-cdj","title":"fcp.1password: Credential Management Connector","description":"## Overview
Secure credential management connector for 1Password integration, enabling AI agents to safely access and manage secrets without exposing sensitive data.

## Core Capabilities

### Item Lookup (Read-Only by Default)
- Search vault items by name, tags, or categories
- Retrieve specific fields (username, URL, notes) without exposing passwords
- Read-only access as the default permission level
- Explicit elevation required for password retrieval

### Password Generation
- Generate strong passwords with configurable requirements
- Support for length, character sets, and complexity rules
- Memorable password generation (word-based)
- PIN generation for numeric-only contexts

### Entry Creation
- Create new login entries with generated passwords
- Support for secure notes, credit cards, identities
- Custom field support for application-specific data
- Automatic categorization and tagging

### Vault Organization
- List and navigate vault structure
- Move items between vaults
- Bulk tagging operations
- Archive and restore items

### Secure Note Access
- Read secure notes for configuration data
- Structured note parsing (YAML, JSON embedded)
- Document attachment retrieval
- Version history access

## Security Requirements

### Credential Protection
- **CRITICAL**: Never expose credentials in logs or output
- Mask all sensitive fields in debug output
- Use secure memory handling
- Clear credentials from memory after use

### Time-Limited Credential Leases
- Short-lived access tokens (5-minute default)
- Automatic credential rotation
- Lease renewal with re-authentication
- Audit trail for all credential access

## Implementation Notes
- Use 1Password CLI (op) or Connect API
- Support both personal and team accounts
- Service account integration for automation
- MFA handling for interactive sessions

## Testing Requirements
- Mock vault for unit tests
- Integration tests with test vault
- Security audit for credential handling
- Penetration testing for leak prevention","status":"closed","priority":3,"issue_type":"feature","created_at":"2026-01-11T15:21:48.804778945Z","created_by":"ubuntu","updated_at":"2026-01-15T07:34:56.145167808Z","closed_at":"2026-01-15T07:34:56.145167808Z","close_reason":"Duplicate of flywheel_connectors-8egw","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0}

The wrapper panics with `fcp_async_core::task::spawn called outside an active runtime` instead of surfacing a typed runtime error. That makes runtime mismatch in downstream crates a process abort rather than a recoverable integration fault.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-iqrb","title":"[FCP2] fcp-cli: Developer + Operator CLI","description":"
## Context Switching for Multiple Meshes

Like kubectl contexts, operators managing multiple meshes need easy switching.

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Deserialize, Serialize)]
pub struct ContextConfig {
    /// Available contexts
    pub contexts: HashMap<String, MeshContext>,
    /// Current active context
    pub current_context: String,
}

#[derive(Debug, Deserialize, Serialize)]
pub struct MeshContext {
    /// Context name (user-defined)
    pub name: String,
    /// Mesh endpoint
    pub endpoint: String,
    /// Default zone for this context
    pub default_zone: Option<String>,
    /// Node identity for this mesh
    pub node_identity: Option<PathBuf>,
    /// Additional context-specific config
    pub config_overrides: HashMap<String, serde_json::Value>,
}

#[derive(Subcommand)]
enum ContextCommands {
    /// List all contexts
    List,
    /// Show current context
    Current,
    /// Switch to a different context
    Use { name: String },
    /// Create a new context
    Create(CreateContextArgs),
    /// Delete a context
    Delete { name: String },
    /// Rename a context
    Rename { old_name: String, new_name: String },
}

#[derive(Args)]
pub struct CreateContextArgs {
    /// Context name
    name: String,
    /// Mesh endpoint
    #[arg(long)]
    endpoint: String,
    /// Default zone
    #[arg(long)]
    zone: Option<String>,
    /// Set as current context
    #[arg(long)]
    set_current: bool,
}

impl ContextCommands {
    pub fn run(&self, config: &mut ContextConfig) -> Result<(), Error> {
        match self {
            Self::List => {
                for (name, ctx) in &config.contexts {
                    let current = if name == &config.current_context { "*" } else { " " };
                    println!("{} {} -> {}", current, name, ctx.endpoint);
                }
            }
            Self::Current => {
                println!("{}", config.current_context);
            }
            Self::Use { name } => {
                if !config.contexts.contains_key(name) {
                    return Err(Error::ContextNotFound(name.clone()));
                }
                config.current_context = name.clone();
                config.save()?;
                println!("Switched to context: {}", name);
            }
            // ... other commands
            _ => todo!()
        }
        Ok(())
    }
}
```

### Context CLI Usage
```bash
# List contexts
fcp context list
#   production -> unix:///var/run/fcp/prod.sock
# * staging    -> unix:///var/run/fcp/staging.sock
#   local      -> unix:///tmp/fcp-dev.sock

# Switch context
fcp context use production
# Switched to context: production

# Create context
fcp context create dev-cluster --endpoint tcp://192.168.1.100:9000 --zone z:dev

# Use context for single command
fcp --context=production doctor

# Delete context
fcp context delete old-cluster
```

## Stdin Input Handling

```rust
#[derive(Parser)]
struct Cli {
    // ... existing fields ...

    /// Read input from stdin (for piping)
    #[arg(long, global = true)]
    input_stdin: bool,

    /// Input format for stdin (json, toml, or raw)
    #[arg(long, global = true, default_value = "json")]
    input_format: InputFormat,
}

#[derive(Clone, ValueEnum)]
pub enum InputFormat {
    Json,
    Toml,
    Raw,
}

pub async fn read_stdin_input(format: InputFormat) -> Result<serde_json::Value, InputError> {
    let mut input = String::new();
    std::io::stdin().read_to_string(&mut input)?;

    match format {
        InputFormat::Json => Ok(serde_json::from_str(&input)?),
        InputFormat::Toml => {
            let toml_value: toml::Value = toml::from_str(&input)?;
            Ok(serde_json::to_value(toml_value)?)
        }
        InputFormat::Raw => Ok(json!({ "raw": input.trim() })),
    }
}

// Usage in commands:
impl DoctorArgs {
    pub async fn run(&self, cli: &Cli) -> Result<(), Error> {
        let input = if cli.input_stdin {
            Some(read_stdin_input(cli.input_format.clone()).await?)
        } else {
            None
        };

        // Use input.zone if provided via stdin
        let zone = input.as_ref()
            .and_then(|i| i["zone"].as_str())
            .or(self.zone.as_deref())
            .ok_or(Error::MissingZone)?;

        // ... rest of command
        todo!()
    }
}
```

```bash
# From file
```

## Pager Support for Long Outputs

```rust
pub struct PagerConfig {
    /// Pager command (default: $PAGER or 'less')
    pub command: String,
    /// Minimum lines before using pager
    pub threshold: usize,
    /// Pager arguments
    pub args: Vec<String>,
    /// Disable pager
    pub disabled: bool,
}

impl Default for PagerConfig {
    fn default() -> Self {
        Self {
            command: std::env::var("PAGER").unwrap_or_else(|_| "less".to_string()),
            threshold: 50,
            args: vec!["-R".to_string()], // -R for color support
            disabled: false,
        }
    }
}

pub fn output_with_pager(content: &str, config: &PagerConfig) -> Result<(), PagerError> {
    let line_count = content.lines().count();

    // Don't use pager if:
    // - Disabled
    // - Not a TTY
    // - Output is short
    // - In JSON mode
    if config.disabled
        || !std::io::stdout().is_terminal()
        || line_count < config.threshold
    {
        print!("{}", content);
        return Ok(());
    }

    // Spawn pager
    let mut child = std::process::Command::new(&config.command)
        .args(&config.args)
        .stdin(std::process::Stdio::piped())
        .spawn()?;

    if let Some(mut stdin) = child.stdin.take() {
        stdin.write_all(content.as_bytes())?;
    }

    child.wait()?;
    Ok(())
}

// CLI flag
#[derive(Parser)]
struct Cli {
    /// Disable pager for long outputs
    #[arg(long, global = true)]
    no_pager: bool,

    /// Force pager even for short outputs
    #[arg(long, global = true)]
    pager: bool,
}
```

### Pager Usage
```bash
# Automatic pager for long outputs
fcp audit tail --zone z:owner --limit 1000

# Disable pager
fcp audit tail --no-pager

# Force pager
fcp doctor --pager

# Environment variable
PAGER="bat --paging=always" fcp explain --request abc123
```

## Offline Operation Mode

```rust
#[derive(Clone, ValueEnum)]
pub enum OperationMode {
    /// Normal online operation
    Online,
    /// Offline mode (use cached data only)
    Offline,
    /// Prefer cache, fallback to network
    CacheFirst,
}

#[derive(Parser)]
struct Cli {
    /// Operation mode (online, offline, cache-first)
    #[arg(long, global = true, default_value = "online")]
    mode: OperationMode,
}

pub struct OfflineCapableCommand<T> {
    /// Online implementation
    online: T,
    /// Offline implementation (may be partial)
    offline: Option<T>,
    /// Cache for offline access
    cache: Cache,
}

impl OfflineCapableCommand<DoctorResult> {
    pub async fn run(&self, mode: OperationMode) -> Result<DoctorResult, Error> {
        match mode {
            OperationMode::Online => self.online.run().await,
            OperationMode::Offline => {
                tracing::info!("Running in offline mode, using cached data");
                self.offline.as_ref()
                    .ok_or(Error::OfflineNotSupported)?
                    .run_offline(&self.cache)
                    .await
            }
            OperationMode::CacheFirst => {
                match self.cache.get_recent() {
                    Some(cached) if cached.is_fresh() => Ok(cached),
                    _ => self.online.run().await,
                }
            }
        }
    }
}

// Commands that support offline mode:
// - explain (if decision is cached)
// - audit show <id> (if event is cached)
// - info <connector> (if manifest is cached)
// - verify <connector> (if binary is cached)

// Commands that require online:
// - doctor (needs live freshness check)
// - repair (needs to contact mesh)
// - audit tail (streaming requires connection)
```

## Command Aliasing

```rust
#[derive(Debug, Deserialize, Default)]
pub struct AliasConfig {
    /// User-defined aliases
    pub aliases: HashMap<String, String>,
}

impl AliasConfig {
    pub fn expand(&self, args: &[String]) -> Vec<String> {
        if args.is_empty() {
            return args.to_vec();
        }

        let command = &args[0];

        // Check for alias
        if let Some(expansion) = self.aliases.get(command) {
            // Split expansion and prepend to remaining args
            let mut expanded: Vec<String> = expansion
                .split_whitespace()
                .map(String::from)
                .collect();
            expanded.extend(args[1..].iter().cloned());
            return expanded;
        }

        args.to_vec()
    }
}
```

## Windows Signal Handling

```rust
#[cfg(windows)]
pub fn setup_signal_handlers() -> Result<SignalState, SignalError> {
    use windows::Win32::System::Console::*;

    let state = Arc::new(SignalState::default());
    let state_clone = state.clone();

    // Windows uses SetConsoleCtrlHandler instead of Unix signals
    unsafe {
        SetConsoleCtrlHandler(Some(console_handler), true)?;
    }

    Ok(state)
}

#[cfg(windows)]
unsafe extern "system" fn console_handler(ctrl_type: u32) -> i32 {
    use windows::Win32::System::Console::*;

    match ctrl_type {
        CTRL_C_EVENT | CTRL_BREAK_EVENT => {
            eprintln!("\nReceived interrupt, shutting down...");
            // Signal shutdown
            1 // Handled
        }
        CTRL_CLOSE_EVENT | CTRL_LOGOFF_EVENT | CTRL_SHUTDOWN_EVENT => {
            // Save state before exit
            1
        }
        _ => 0, // Not handled
    }
}

// No SIGHUP on Windows, but we can use file watching for config reload
#[cfg(windows)]
pub fn setup_config_watcher(config_path: &Path, config: Arc<RwLock<FcpConfig>>) {
    use notify::{Watcher, RecursiveMode, watcher};

    let (tx, rx) = std::sync::mpsc::channel();
    let mut watcher = watcher(tx, Duration::from_secs(2)).unwrap();

    watcher.watch(config_path, RecursiveMode::NonRecursive).unwrap();

    std::thread::spawn(move || {
        for event in rx {
            if let Ok(notify::Event { kind: notify::EventKind::Modify(_), .. }) = event {
                tracing::info!("Config file changed, reloading");
                // Reload config
            }
        }
    });
}
```

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-iqrb.1","title":"CLI: fcp install (verify + mirror)","description":"# CLI: `fcp install` (verify + mirror)

## Goal
Implement `fcp install` as the user-facing entrypoint for installing a connector safely.

## Behavior
- Fetch or load a connector manifest + binary.
- Run the full verification chain (policy-controlled):
  - manifest signature(s)
  - binary checksum and signature
  - platform/arch compatibility
  - policy requirements (transparency/attestations when required)
- Mirror/pin connector objects into the mesh store for offline installs.
- Emit audit events describing what was installed (by object id).

## Output
- Human mode: concise success/failure.
- `--json` mode: machine-readable result including installed object ids.

## Tests
- Integration tests with a mock registry:
  - install success
  - bad signature rejected
  - checksum mismatch rejected
  - missing attestation rejected when policy requires
- Snapshot tests for `--json` output.

## Acceptance Criteria
- A connector cannot be installed if verification fails.
- CLI never prints secrets.

## Goal
Implement `fcp doctor --zone <zone>` to report the current safety posture of a zone.

Report, at minimum:
- latest pinned ZoneCheckpoint id + checkpoint_seq + age
- revocation/audit head freshness state
- whether the system is in degraded mode for this zone and why
- transport policy settings (DERP/Funnel allowed?)
- store coverage summary for key roots (checkpoint, policy heads)

## Output
- Human mode: clear OK/WARN/FAIL summary with reason codes.
- `--json` mode: full structured state for automation.

## Tests
- E2E harness scenarios:
  - fresh checkpoint -> OK
  - stale checkpoint -> WARN/FAIL depending on policy
  - missing checkpoint -> degraded mode report

## Acceptance Criteria
- Output is sufficient to decide whether Risky/Dangerous operations should be allowed.

 {"id":"flywheel_connectors-q4kuy","title":"[REVIEW-A9-FUZZ][P2] fcp-streaming/websocket.rs: missing fuzz target for WS frame opcode/length/payload parser","description":"crates/fcp-streaming/src/websocket.rs has no fuzz target. WebSocket frame decode runs on attacker-controlled bytes after the (much shorter) HTTP upgrade handshake — this is the classic place for length-prefix overflow, masking-key reuse, fragmentation reassembly, and control-frame-mixed-with-message bugs.

fuzzable input shape:

fuzz_target!(|data: &[u8]| {
    // Cap input to 1 MiB so per-iteration cost stays under the libFuzzer
    // throughput floor; the parser's MAX_PAYLOAD_LEN cap should fire
    // long before we get there.
    if data.len() > 1024 * 1024 { return; }
    let mut decoder = WsFrameDecoder::new(WsConfig::default());
    let _ = decoder.feed(data); // never panic
});

frame fields the parser consumes from attacker bytes:
- FIN/RSV1-3 flag bits (4 bits)
- opcode (4 bits): 0x0 continuation, 0x1 text, 0x2 binary, 0x8 close, 0x9 ping, 0xA pong
- MASK flag (1 bit) + length (7 bits + extended 16/64-bit forms)
- masking key (4 bytes when MASK=1)
- payload (up to 64-bit length declared by the previous fields)

properties to exercise:
- 64-bit length over MAX_PAYLOAD_LEN must reject without allocating
- continuation-frame sequences without an initial text/binary frame must reject
- control frame (close/ping/pong) of >125 bytes must reject (RFC 6455 §5.5)
- close frame status code is required to be 2 bytes if any payload (don't accept 1-byte status)
- masking key reuse across fragments is allowed by spec but a fuzzer can probe XOR boundary conditions
- interleaved control + data frames must not mis-deliver a control payload to the data buffer

priority rationale: P2. WebSocket frames are framed binary, so the parser is the most exposed code in fcp-streaming. Unlike SSE (which is line-oriented and forgiving), WS framing has known historical CVEs around extension negotiation + length-extension attacks.  {"id":"flywheel_connectors-qgu6t","title":"[CRITICAL] Fix non-production default URLs in DocuSign and Plaid connectors","status":"closed","priority":0,"issue_type":"task","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:12.695374Z","created_by":"jemanuel","updated_at":"2026-03-25T03:46:53.020397Z","closed_at":"2026-03-25T03:46:53.020226Z","close_reason":"done","source_repo":".","compaction_level":0,"original_size":0,"labels":["connectors","critical","hardening"],"dependencies":[{"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-qgu6t","depends_on_id":"flywheel_connectors-1z643","type":"blocks","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:19.245594Z","created_by":"jemanuel","metadata":"{}","thread_id":""}],"comments":[{"id":1938,"issue_id":"flywheel_connectors-qgu6t","author":"jemanuel","text":"CRITICAL: Non-Production Default URLs

Two connectors ship DEFAULT_BASE_URL constants pointing at non-production environments. Any deployment using the out-of-the-box defaults will silently hit sandbox/demo infrastructure rather than the provider's production API. This is the most dangerous class of hard-coded constant because it fails silently — requests succeed but against the wrong data.

FINDINGS:

1. DocuSign (connectors/docusign/src/client.rs:38):
   DEFAULT_BASE_URL = "https://demo.docusign.net/restapi/v2.1/accounts"
   - This is the DocuSign DEMO environment, not production
   - Production URLs follow the pattern: https://na1.docusign.net/..., https://eu.docusign.net/..., etc.
   - The connector's doctor output (connector.rs:1050) mentions both demo and production patterns, but the *default* is demo
   - Any user who doesn't explicitly override base_url gets demo

2. Plaid (connectors/plaid/src/client.rs:21):
   DEFAULT_BASE_URL = "https://sandbox.plaid.com"
   - This is the Plaid SANDBOX environment, not production or development
   - Plaid has three environments: sandbox, development, production
   - All data in sandbox is fake test data
   - The connector explicitly names environments (connector.rs:18,22,65) but defaults to sandbox

FIX APPROACH:
- Remove the hard-coded default entirely from both connectors
- Make base_url a required configuration field (no default = forces explicit choice)
- Add environment enum (Production/Sandbox/Demo) to the configure() handler
- Update doctor/self_check to warn if running against non-production
- Update manifest.toml to document the required configuration
- Add tests verifying that unconfigured connectors refuse to start rather than silently hitting demo/sandbox

WHY THIS MATTERS:
FCP's security model is zone-based with capability tokens. A connector that silently routes traffic to a demo server undermines the entire trust chain — real capability tokens are sent to a test environment, potentially leaking production intent to sandbox infrastructure. This is a category of bug that the FCP security invariants should mechanically prevent.","created_at":"2026-03-25T03:05:36Z"}]}
